verbs.summary: λελάληκα is a Greek perfect active indicative, 1st person singular. In the main clause (“ταῦτα λελάληκα ὑμῖν”), it presents the speaking/telling as a completed action in the past with ongoing relevance, with the indicative mood asserting the statement as factual (“I have told you these things”). Its role is the finite verb that anchors the sentence.

ἔχητε is a Greek present active subjunctive, 2nd person plural. It occurs in the purpose/clause introduced by ἵνα (“ἵνα ἐν ἐμοὶ εἰρήνην ἔχητε”), where the subjunctive expresses intended outcome or purpose. Semantically, it means that the addressees should/are meant to have peace in the speaker (“you may have peace in me”). The mood and ἵνα make it subordinate, functioning as the verb inside the ἵνα clause.

ἔχετε is a Greek present active indicative, 2nd person plural. In “ἐν τῷ κόσμῳ θλῖψιν ἔχετε,” it states a present, general reality: “you have tribulation in the world.” The indicative mood marks it as a straightforward assertion, and the verb serves as the finite predicate of its clause.

θαρσεῖτε is a Greek present active imperative, 2nd person plural. In “ἀλλὰ θαρσεῖτε,” it introduces a direct command/encouragement contrasting with what precedes (“but take courage”). The imperative mood marks it as an exhortation rather than a statement of fact, and the present tense gives the command an ongoing/durable sense: “keep on being encouraged / take courage (now).”

νενίκηκα is a Greek perfect active indicative, 1st person singular. In “ἐγὼ νενίκηκα τὸν κόσμον,” it declares the speaker’s victory as a completed act with continuing effect (“I have overcome the world”). The indicative mood presents this as certain truth, and the verb is the finite predicate that forms the final assertion in its clause.
